title: Critical minerals
subtitle: Who digs, refines and separates the minerals everything else is built from, and who cannot route around whom.
financials_note: Five-year income-statement trends for the chokepoints. Revenue bars read on the left; margins read on the right. Note that this map's chokepoints are largely Chinese A-share filers, so expect lower source tiers here than in a map of Western filers, and read the tier badge before trusting a number.
chokepoints_note: The companies the rest of the chain has the fewest alternatives to, ranked by market value. Mining is comparatively diverse; separation, refining and conversion are where the routing options run out.

layers.label: Miners
layers.desc: Pull the ore out of the ground

layers.label: Refiners
layers.desc: Turn ore into usable metal and chemicals

layers.label: Separators
layers.desc: Split mixed rare earths into single elements

layers.label: Battery chemical makers
layers.desc: Convert metal into cathode and anode chemicals

layers.label: Magnet makers
layers.desc: Sinter rare earths into permanent magnets

layers.label: Recyclers
layers.desc: Recover metal from scrap and spent cells

chokepoint_why.China Northern Rare Earth (Group) High-T: The world's largest light rare earth producer; its transfer price sets China's domestic benchmark.
chokepoint_why.JL MAG Rare-Earth: One of the largest sintered NdFeB magnet makers, in the country that does most of the sintering.
chokepoint_why.Ganfeng Lithium Group: A top lithium converter; Australian spodumene still largely becomes battery chemicals in China.
chokepoint_why.Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt: Sits on the ore-to-chemical handoff that most DRC cobalt passes through.
chokepoint_why.Yunnan Chihong Zinc & Germanium: A leading refiner of germanium, a metal China has used export licensing to control.
